title: Enhanced superconductivity in atomically thin noble metals: From quantum confinement to interface-induced Lifshitz transition
authors: Chun-Jie Zhang, Bing Zhang, Yapeng Wu, Xiao-Ping Li, Lei Wang
abstract: Unlocking superconductivity in intrinsically non-superconducting noble metals (Au, Ag, Cu) represents a fundamental challenge in low-dimensional physics. While quantum confinement in the atomically thin limit is known to trigger emergent superconductivity, strategies to amplify this marginal effect to experimentally accessible temperatures remain a key open question. Using first-principles calculations, we establish a unified framework linking intrinsic confinement effects with interface engineering in noble metal films. We reveal that intrinsic superconductivity is element-specific: it is suppressed in Ag by a stiff phonon spectrum, but emerges in trilayer Cu ($T_{\rm C} \approx 0.78$ K) and pentalayer Au ($0.63$ K) driven by confinement-induced density-of-states (DOS) enhancement and phonon softening, respectively. In h-BN/Cu(111) heterostructures, $T_{\rm C}$ is critically dictated by the interfacial stacking configuration. We identify the thermodynamically stable N-bonded interface as a reliable platform for accessible superconductivity ($T_{\rm C} \approx 3.23$ K), whereas manipulating the system into a metastable B-bonded configuration boosts $T_{\rm C}$ to $7.00$ K. This enhancement originates from a B-bonded-induced Lifshitz transition, where the Fermi surface forms a tangential contact with the Brillouin zone boundary at the M point, enhancing electron-phonon coupling beyond DOS effects. Our work unifies the understanding of intrinsic two-dimensional superconductivity with atomistic interface design, offering a blueprint for functionalizing noble metals as emergent superconductors.
summary: This theoretical study develops a unified framework to understand superconductivity in atomically thin noble metals by combining quantum confinement effects with precise interface engineering. The key finding is that the highest superconducting critical temperatures are achieved not merely by increasing electron density, but by engineering the interface to induce a Lifshitz transition, which fundamentally reshapes the Fermi surface and maximizes electron-phonon coupling.
main_problem: The primary challenge is unlocking and enhancing intrinsic superconductivity in noble metals (Au, Ag, Cu), which are fundamentally non-superconducting in their bulk form.
main_result: Superconductivity can be significantly enhanced by engineering interfaces, specifically by inducing a Lifshitz transition in the Fermi surface, which is more effective than simple quantum confinement or charge transfer.
method: The study employs first-principles calculations (DFT/DFPT) to model electronic structure, phonon dispersion, and electron-phonon coupling (EPC) to estimate the superconducting critical temperature (T_C).
model_or_system: The research focuses on atomically thin noble metal films (Au, Ag, Cu) and their heterostructures, particularly those involving hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) interfaces.
key_observables: Superconducting critical temperature (T_C), Electron-Phonon Coupling constant (lambda), Density of States (DOS) at the Fermi level, and the presence/absence of an interface-induced Lifshitz transition.
important_parameters: Film thickness (1L to 7L), interfacial stacking configuration (N-bonded vs. B-bonded), and the electronic topology of the Fermi surface.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on DFT approximations and the McMillan-Allen-Dynes formula for T_C estimation, while also comparing results against simplified analytical models.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate crystal structures for various film thicknesses and heterostructures, showing the evolution of electronic and superconducting properties, and mapping the k-space distribution of electron-phonon coupling for different interface types.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the problem of intrinsic superconductivity in noble metals, then analyzes the thickness dependence of superconductivity in pure films (Au, Cu, Ag). The core of the work compares different interface configurations (h-BN/Cu), demonstrating that the B-site interface promotes a Lifshitz transition, leading to superior T_C compared to N-site or pristine films.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter superconductivity, the mechanism relies on controlling the electronic band structure topology (Lifshitz transition) via atomic interfaces, which is a concept relevant to designing quantum materials and controlling electronic states in nanoscale devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Crystal structures. (a) FCC lattice with the (111) plane and [111] direction. (b) ABC-stacked M (M = Au, Ag, Cu)films with 1 to 5 layers. (c) Side views of h-BN/3L-Cu(111) heterostructures with the interface at the (i) B-site and (ii) N-site. (d) Side views of h-BN/h- BN/3L-Cu(111) heterostructures with AB/BA-stacked (+P/-P) bilayer h-BN on the (i, ii) B-site and (iii, iv) N-site, respectively. (e) Side views of h-BN/3L-Cu(111)/h-BN sandwich heterostructures with the interface at the (i) B-site and (ii) N-site.

Figure 2. Thickness-dependent electronic and superconducting properties. (a-d) Band structures and orbital-resolved DOS of Cu thin films with different thicknesses: monolayer (1L), trilayer (3L), pentalayer (5L), and heptalayer (7L). (e) Superconducting properties of Au, Ag and Cu thin films as a function of layer number: DOS at the Fermi level N(εF), the logarithmic average phonon frequency ωlog, and the electron- phonon coupling constant λ.

Figure 3. Phonon properties and superconductivity. (a–c) Phonon and EPC properties of trilayer Ag, trilayer Cu, and pentalayer Au thin films. For each: EPC-weighted phonon dispersion (red, scaled by λqν), phonon density of states (PhDOS), Eliashberg function α2F(ω), and cumulative EPC λ(ω). (d) Superconducting transition temperature TC versus layer number for Au, Ag, and Cu, plotted with distinct y-axis scales.

Figure 4. Interface-configuration-tuned electronic and EPC properties in h-BN/3L-Cu(111) heterostructure. (a, b) Differential charge density plots for B-site and N-site interfaces in side view. (c, d) Orbital-projected band structures for B-site and N-site, respectively. (e, f) Corres- ponding EPC properties: λqv-scaled phonon dispersions (red), phonon density of states, Eliashberg function α2F(ω), and cumulative λ(ω).The rightmost panels in (e) and (f) show the vibrational eigenmodes associated with the 11 th phonon branch.

Figure 5. k-dependent electron–phonon coupling matrix element, Fermi surface nesting function, and Fermi surface. (a) Electron–phonon coupling matrix element |gnm,v=8(k, 0)| for the 8th phonon mode at the Γ point in the 3L-Cu(111) system. (b, c) Electron–phonon coupling matrix element |gnm,v=11(k, 0)| for the 11th phonon mode at the Γ point in the h-BN/3L-Cu(111) heterostructure (b: B-site interface; c: N-site interface). The color scale in this region represents the magnitude of the matrix element g (in meV). (d) Fermi surface nesting function for the 3L-Cu(111) system. (e, f) Fermi surface nesting function for the h-BN/3L-Cu(111) heterostructure (e: B-site interface; f: N-site interface)....
